one_line_takeaway: Potion combines an outlier-resistant importance estimator (XLF) with a proxy-based hyperparameter search (PTN) to remove backdoor triggers from trained vision models given only a subset of the poison, healing 93.72% of poison at just -1.41% clean-accuracy damage and beating both SSD and full retraining.
key_finding: On the Goel et al. (2024) CIFAR benchmark, Potion (XLF/PTN) heals 93.72% of poison vs 83.41% for the SSD baseline (as reported in Goel et al.) and only 40.68% for full retraining (EU), while cutting the unlearning-induced model-accuracy drop from -5.68% (SSD) to -1.41% (XLF). That is a +12.36% relative poison-removal gain and a -75.18% reduction in model damage over the prior SOTA (XLF's damage is 24.82% of SSD's). Both components help independently: PTN vs Goel's exhaustive SSD search gives +4.93% healing at 46.13% of the damage; XLF vs LF gives +1.33% healing at 53.82% of the damage. Potion also beats the SSD baseline on the never-before-tested One-Shot Healing task (|Sf|=1): 68.01% healed vs 18.24% (SSD effectively fails). Generalisation holds on ViT: on Imagenette BadNet XLF heals 97.28% at -0.51% damage; the sinusoidal trigger is hardest (XLF 80.78% on Imagenette). Compute is practical: XLF takes 11.29s on CIFAR10 vs 141.10s for full retraining.
core_contribution: The paper (Potion) advances 'corrective machine unlearning' for data-poisoning removal, i.e. scrubbing a backdoor/poison trigger out of an already-trained model when only a SUBSET of the poisoned samples has been discovered. It makes two distinct contributions of its own, built on top of the cited Selective Synaptic Dampening (SSD, Foster et al. 2024a) and its Loss-Free variant (LF, Foster et al. 2024b): (1) XLF, an outlier-resistant parameter-importance estimator that uses the l2 norm directly (w=1) instead of the SQUARED l2 norm of the model output used by LF, thereby shortening the heavy tails of near-zero importance values that otherwise cause wrong parameters to be selected for dampening and damage the model; and (2) PTN (Poison Trigger Neutralisation) search, a fast, parallelisable hyperparameter search that tunes SSD's alpha threshold using the accuracy on the DISCOVERED poison set Sf as a proxy for the unknown full poison set Sm, plus an 'over-forgetting buffer' rho that stops unlearning before the model is over-damaged. Combining the two yields Potion (denoted XLF/PTN in the tables). The paper also introduces a new 'One-Shot Healing' task (|Sf|=1) and extends the Goel et al. (2024) benchmark to a ViT architecture, additional datasets (Imagenette, ImageNet-1k), and additional attacks (sine-wave and moving triggers).
threat_model: Adversary poisons a fraction of the training data (the paper follows Goel et al. 2024's motivation of foundation models trained on web-crawled data, citing Carlini et al. 2024 on the feasibility of poisoning crawled datasets). The poison inserts a backdoor trigger, e.g. a BadNet white-pixel patch that redirects images to class zero, or a stop-sign-to-drive attack in the autonomous-driving illustration. The defender is a model owner who can only DETECT a subset Sf of the poisoned set Sm (|Sf| < |Sm|); the retain set therefore stays contaminated with Sm\Sf. The defender must remove the trigger's influence from an already-trained model. Crucially, full retraining from scratch after deleting Sf FAILS because the undiscovered poison reintroduces the trigger, and the defender does not know |Sm| so cannot verify complete removal or tune hyperparameters against ground truth.
limitations: 1) The paper itself notes challenge (1) is inescapable: if |Sf|<|Sm|, complete-removal cannot be verified by the unlearning method alone; it requires better data-discovery methods. 2) Performance is sensitive to rho: too low (< the ~10% of samples whose poison label already equals the clean label) forces the model to be damaged to hit 0% Sf accuracy, and too high (>=50%) stops before the trigger is removed. 3) The method does not handle label-switching poison, which the authors deliberately exclude as misaligned with unlearning (no common trigger to unlearn). 4) On the moving-trigger attack no method (including Potion) achieves one-shot healing, and on ImageNet-1k sinusoidal, SSD actually heals more (75.74% vs XLF 68.00%) though at unusable -6.20% damage. 5) Scope is vision classification (CNN/ViT, CIFAR/Imagenette/ImageNet) with backdoor-style triggers; no NLP/LLM or non-image results beyond a cited tabular case study. 6) The strong headline numbers depend on the healing metric being defined relative to full retraining, and results converge between XLF and LF on larger over-parameterised models, shrinking XLF's marginal benefit.

quantitative_results: Poison healed on Goel et al. CIFAR benchmark: XLF (Potion) 93.72±1.99%, LF 92.49±3.58%, SSD-with-PTN 87.52±11.18%, SSD baseline (Goel) 83.41±14.07%, EU/full-retrain 40.68±32.16%, None 18.24%
Model damage (clean test-acc drop) on same benchmark: XLF -1.41±0.92%, LF -2.62±4.01%, SSD baseline -5.68±4.42%, EU +0.04±0.33%
Relative gain over SOTA: +12.36% poison removal; -75.18% model damage (XLF damage = 24.82% of SSD's)
Ablation PTN vs Goel exhaustive SSD search: +4.93% healing at 46.13% of the damage; XLF vs LF: +1.33% healing at 53.82% of the damage
One-Shot Healing (|Sf|=1) CIFAR: XLF 68.01±14.61% healed at -1.34% damage vs SSD 18.24% (fails)
ViT Imagenette BadNet: XLF 97.28±1.45% healed, -0.51% damage; sinusoidal: XLF 80.78±9.33%; moving trigger: XLF 97.67±2.00% healed, -1.55% damage
ViT ImageNet-1k BadNet: XLF 70.83±9.94% healed at -0.36% damage; sinusoidal: SSD 75.74% (but -6.20% damage) vs XLF 68.00% (-0.71% damage)
Compute (|Sm|=500 CIFAR10): XLF 11.29±2.12s, LF 13.41±3.90s, SSD single run 4.42±0.13s, full retrain (EU) 141.10±1.38s
sstep sensitivity: sstep=2.0 gives XLF 95.64% healed / -1.83% damage; sstep=1.01 gives 92.73% / -1.31%
Potion hyperparameters: rho=20%, bstart=25, sstep=1.1; BadNet trigger = 0.3% white pixels redirecting to class 0; poison sizes |Sm|=[100,500,1000] = 0.2%/1%/2%
baselines_compared: SSD (Selective Synaptic Dampening, Foster et al. 2024a) as tuned by Goel et al. 2024
SSD combined with the paper's own PTN search
EU (Exact Unlearning / full retraining from scratch on Str\Sf)
Cited-but-not-rerun failing methods: SCRUB (Kurmanji et al. 2024), Bad Teacher (Chundawat et al. 2023)
